full_response:
We want to show that, inside one and the same coordinate chart {x^μ}, the Jacobian matrix of the transformation “from the coordinates to themselves’’ is the identity, i.e.  

∂x^μ / ∂x^ν = δ^μ_ν .                                                      (1)

Here δ^μ_ν is the Kronecker delta, equal to 1 if μ = ν and 0 otherwise.  
Below we make this result completely explicit.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 1:  Regard x^μ as functions on the manifold
--------------------------------------------------------------------
In any smooth space-time manifold M we introduce a single coordinate chart

x :  M  →  ℝ⁴ ,      p ↦ (x⁰(p), x¹(p), x²(p), x³(p)).            (2)

Thus each coordinate function x^μ is a scalar function on M.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 2:  Form the “identity’’ coordinate transformation
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Consider now the (trivial) change of coordinates that re-labels points by the same numbers:

x'^μ  ≡  x^μ .                                                     (3)

Since x'^μ are obtained only by renaming, this is the identity map in ℝ⁴.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 3:  Differentiate using ordinary partial derivatives
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Treating the coordinates as independent variables, compute the Jacobian of (3):

∂x'^μ
───────  =  ∂x^μ / ∂x^ν .                                           (4)
∂x^ν

But because x'^μ is exactly x^μ, every coordinate depends only on itself:

• If μ = ν, the derivative of x^μ with respect to x^μ is 1.  
• If μ ≠ ν, the derivative of x^μ with respect to x^ν is 0,

since x^μ does not depend on x^ν when ν ≠ μ.  Symbolically,

∂x^μ / ∂x^ν  =  { 1  (μ = ν)
                 { 0  (μ ≠ ν)                                   (5)

and this piecewise definition is precisely the Kronecker delta.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 4:  Collect the result
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Therefore, for any single coordinate system {x^μ},

∂x^μ
────── = δ^μ_ν .                                                    (6)
∂x^ν

Equation (6) expresses that the Jacobian of the identity transformation is the identity matrix, a result used ubiquitously in tensor calculus and relativity.

\boxed{\displaystyle \frac{\partial x^{\mu}}{\partial x^{\nu}} = \delta^{\mu}_{\ \nu}}

Confidence: 95%
